bids.reasoning: This is the opening offer at the very beginning of the negotiation (t=0.0). I will bid my most preferred value for every issue to establish a strong anchor and claim maximum utility (1.0). I can evaluate the opponent's preferences and find mutually beneficial concessions in later rounds based on their counteroffers.

It is extremely early in the negotiation (t=0.02), so I should keep my utility target high. The opponent's message is aggressive cheap talk intended to force early capitulation, but their own offer gave me minimal utility. I will concede slightly on my lowest-weighted issue (issueA) to show movement and maintain my top choices on the high-weight issues (issueC and issueD).

The negotiation is in its very early stages (t=0.04). The opponent has refused to concede, repeating their starting bid while adopting an aggressive stance and using insulting language as cheap talk to extract concessions. I will ignore their rhetoric and maintain steady, incremental steps by making a small concession on my lowest-priority issue, issueA, going from valueC to valueB, while holding firm on the more critical parameters.

The opponent continues to bid their maximum value (all valueA) without offering any concessions, while sending aggressive messages to blindly pressure me. As it is still early in the negotiation (t=0.06), I will ignore the aggressive rhetoric and make a strategic concession on my lowest-weight issue (issueA). Yielding on issueA completely to match their bid leaves my high-priority issues intact and signals a genuine willingness to collaborate if they begin making serious concessions too.

The negotiation is still in its very early stages (t=0.08), but the opponent has aggressively held firm on their opening position without making any concessions. Because I have already fully yielded on my lowest-weighted issue (issueA), I will make a very small concession on my next lowest-weighted issue (issueB) from valueD to valueC to continue showing a willingness to compromise. However, I will strongly retain my ideal values on my two most critical issues (issueC and issueD) and wait for the opponent to reciprocate.

The opponent's aggressive messages are clearly a negotiating tactic (cheap talk), designed to pressure me without making meaningful concessions themselves. At t=0.10, they finally moved slightly on issueD, but their overall offers remain very low in utility for me. Because we are still very early in the negotiation (t=0.1), I will ignore the false urgency and insults, and simply continue making measured, systematic down-steps on my least important issues (issueB) while holding firm on my most important ones (issueC and issueD).

The current time is very early (t=0.12). The opponent is employing an aggressively confrontational emotional strategy ("insulting", "lowballing", "garbage") while making only very minor concessions on issueD. I will ignore the opponent's "cheap talk" and focus on finding a mutually beneficial configuration. Since my utility for issueA and issueB is the lowest, I will explore a lateral move: conceding fully on issueB to test if it yields them higher value, while maintaining my highly valued positions on issueC and issueD.

It is still early in the negotiation (t=0.14), but the opponent remains highly aggressive and extremely anchored, barely conceding on issueD to valueB. Since my priority dictates protecting issueC and issueD, I will hold completely firm on those while rotating my concessions on the less critical issues (issueA and issueB) to search for acceptable trade-offs without significantly jeopardizing my utility. I am offering a mix of values I haven't combined yet to see if they prefer concessions on issueA or issueB.

The negotiation is still in the early stages (t=0.16), leaving plenty of time to find a mutually acceptable agreement. The opponent has employed aggressive language, which functions as tactical cheap talk, but functionally they have only made minor probing concessions on issueD and issueC. To continue exploring the trade-off space while protecting my core interests, I will offer a moderate concession on my lower-priority issues (issueA and issueB) and hold firm on my highest-priority issues (issueC and issueD) to test their flexibility.

The negotiation is still in the early stages (t=0.18), and the opponent relies on aggressive messaging tactics while making very slow, small concessions on issues D and C. Since my highest priorities are issueD (weight 0.4) and issueC (weight 0.3), I must maintain valueD on those issues. I will offer a moderate concession combination on my low-weight issues A and B to maintain my utility around 0.89 while testing their willingness to accept alternative trade-offs.

I am navigating the early-mid stage (t=0.20) of negotiation and tracking the opponent's behavior. Their aggressive messages contrast with their incremental concessions across different issues (Value B for issues D, C, and now B). I will maintain strong utility around 0.84 by holding firm on my most important issue (issueD), but making my first concession on my second-most important issue (issueC) to see how they respond.

The opponent's strategy combines aggressively negative messages with very marginal, alternating concessions on single issues, keeping mostly towards their apparent top choices. As we are still early in the negotiation (t=0.22), I will maintain a relatively high utility for myself (~0.86). To find a mutually acceptable combination, I am rotating my compromises, this time conceding partially on my lower-weighted `issueA` and moderately on `issueC`, while remaining firm on my highest priority `issueD`.

The opponent has shown willingness to concede on issueD (recently offering valueC) and sporadically on other issues. Since issueD is my most important issue (weight 0.4), I will hold firm on valueD for maximum utility. To move the negotiation forward and respond to their signals, I will offer a concession on issueB (from valueD to valueC) while keeping a moderate stance on issueA and issueC, ignoring their aggressive messages as cheap talk.

The opponent continues to rely on aggressive messaging and slow, minor concessions, treating their lowest offers as significant moves. With the time fraction at 0.26, I am willing to offer further concessions on issues A and B where my utility weights are lower (0.1 and 0.2, respectively), specifically granting their apparent preferred value for B (`valueA`). This brings my utility down to around 0.787 while firmly protecting issues C and D where my stakes are highest.

The opponent is aggressively accusing me of lowballing but has been making extremely small shifts in their own offers across different issues. Given the time fraction (0.28), I will concede fully on my two lowest-weighted issues (A and B) by offering "valueA" to match the opponent's persistent requests for those, while holding firm on my most important issues (C and D) at "valueD" to maintain a solid utility of 0.76. I am ignoring the opponent's aggressive rhetoric as standard cheap talk.

At t=0.3, we are still moderately early in the negotiation. I am targeting an offer with a utility around 0.78. I am holding firm on issueD because it is my highest priority (weight 0.4), while proposing intermediate values for issueA, issueB, and issueC to probe the opponent's true preferences without yielding too much overall utility. The opponent's hostile messages are ignored as cheap-talk tactics; I base my decisions strictly on their bidding history, which shows only minor exploration across the issues.

The opponent is repeatedly using aggressive and insulting language, which is a known cheap-talk tactic. I will ignore these emotional appeals and focus on the bids. Given that the time fraction is still early-mid game (0.32), I will maintain a relatively high utility for myself (~0.78) by holding firm on my most important issue (issueD) while testing another combination of concessions on issueA, issueB, and issueC to see if it aligns better with their unstated preferences.

I am balancing my top-priority issues (issueD and issueC) with finding compromises on my lower-weighted issues (issueA and issueB). Despite the opponent's aggressive messages, I am looking at their actual bidding behavior, which shows slight variations but heavily guards their favorites. Thus, I am holding my highest priority issueD at valueD while offering a blend of valueB and valueC on the other issues to maintain an acceptable utility (~0.76) without dipping too low at this stage (t=0.34).

The opponent's messages consist of a repeated, aggressive script accusing me of "lowballing," which is a common negotiation tactic to induce panic concessions; I will ignore the rhetoric and focus on their bid patterns. They have occasionally softened on `issueC` (bidding `valueB` and `valueC`), while strongly anchoring on `issueA`, `issueB`, and `issueD` for their `valueA`. Since we are at `t=0.36`, I have room to hold firm on my highest-priority `issueD` (weight 0.4) while cautiously exploring tradeoffs on `issueA` and `issueC` to see if we can find common ground. My new bid maintains a solid utility around 0.78 for me.

The opponent's messages are consistently aggressive but simply constitute cheap talk to force concessions, so I will ignore the rhetoric and focus on their bidding behavior. They persistently demand `valueA` for `issueA` and `issueD`, while showing flexibility on `issueB` and `issueC` (recently proposing `valueC` for both). To protect my highest-weighted issue (`issueD`), I will hold firm on `valueD` but concede to their preferred `valueA` on `issueA` (my lowest-weighted issue). I will balance the rest of the offer by proposing `valueB` for `issueB` and `valueC` for `issueC`, keeping my utility near 0.73 at this early-mid stage.

I am completely ignoring the opponent's aggressive messages as they are cheap talk aimed at forcing premature concessions. The opponent has repeatedly offered `valueA` and sometimes `valueB` or `valueC` across various issues. Since `issueA` and `issueB` are my lowest-weighted issues, I am conceding them fully to `valueA` (which the opponent favors), while maintaining a slightly compromised `valueC` for `issueC` and holding firm on my most important issue, `issueD` at `valueD`. This targets a utility around 0.68 for me as we approach mid-game.

The opponent's messages remain aggressive, but their actual offers show some flexibility on issues C and D. Since time is approaching the middle of the negotiation (t=0.42), I will ignore the harsh cheap talk and make a targeted concession. I am offering the exact same values they requested in their last offer for issues A, B, and C, while holding firm on my most important issue, issueD (valueD), resulting in a utility of 0.655 for myself.

I am holding firm on `issueD` at my maximum value (`valueD`) because it is my most heavily weighted issue (0.4 importance). The opponent's messages are aggressive, but they are cheap talk and have proven flexible on `issueA` and `issueB` (both valueB in their last offer). To show cooperation while protecting my utility, I am matching their willingness to accept `valueB` on my lower-weight issues, while continuing to concede slightly on `issueC`.

The opponent is employing a tactic of aggressive messaging, calling every offer "insulting" to push for rapid concessions. However, looking at the actual numbers, they are exploring different trade-offs and have recently shown flexibility on issueB (offering valueC) and issueD (offering valueB). Consequently, I will target a utility of ~0.71 by aligning with their recent concession on issueB (valueC) while prioritizing my highest-weight issue, issueD (valueD). I will also offer their frequently proposed valueA for issueA, and find a middle ground on issueC (valueB).

The opponent is using a consistent aggressive communication strategy, asserting my offers are "insulting" as a cheap talk tactic to force concessions, regardless of my actual compromises. I will ignore the rhetoric and focus on the numbers. At t=0.48, I am targeting a utility around 0.70. Since issueD is my most critical priority (weight 0.4), I am firmly holding valueD there while making modest concessions on issueA, issueB, and issueC based on the values the opponent has recently requested.

The opponent's latest offer gives ground on issues B, C, and D, reaching `valueB`, `valueC`, and `valueB` respectively. Given the 0.5 time fraction, I will start matching their moves on less important issues while maintaining a strong position on my highest weighted issue. By offering `valueA` on issueA, `valueB` on issueB, and `valueC` on issueC, I am accommodating their preferences to a significant degree while firmly holding `valueD` on my most critical issue (issue D) to secure a utility of 0.733.

The opponent's latest offer introduces a concession on `issueB` to `valueD`, while resetting other issues. Since `issueB` yields maximum utility for me at `valueD`, I will incorporate this into my bid. To reciprocate, I will concede moderately on `issueC` to `valueB`, while keeping `issueA` at their strongly preferred `valueA` and maintaining my most important issue, `issueD`, at its highest utility to protect my baseline. This maintains a strong utility while aligning with the opponent's recent explorations.

I am maintaining my focus on maximizing `issueD` and `issueC` as they carry the most weight for my utility (0.4 and 0.3 respectively). The opponent has been consistently aggressive with their text messages, but their actual offers show some flexibility on `issueB` and `issueC`. I will accommodate them by conceding `issueA` entirely and offering a moderate concession on `issueB` and `issueC`, while continuing to hold firm on `issueD`.

The opponent's messages consist of repetitive and highly aggressive template texts, which I will ignore to focus strictly on their bidding behavior. We've matched on issueA (my lowest value, their apparent highest) and have been circling similar middle-ground values for issueB and issueC. Since we have reached the midpoint of the negotiation, I will keep conceding issueA to their preferred value while maintaining issueD (my highest priority) at my maximum. I will offer a mix for issueB and issueC that provides a compromise without dropping my overall utility too sharply.

The opponent continually complains of "lowballing" through repetitive aggressive messages, which I ignore as a known tactic. Looking purely at their offers, they have recently proposed `issueB: valueD` (t=0.52, 0.58) and `issueC: valueC` (t=0.56). I will match their preference for `issueA: valueA` and combine it with `issueB: valueD`, retaining `valueC` on issueC and `valueD` on my most important issue (issueD) to target a strong utility of ~0.84.

The opponent's repetitive messages are cheap talk, so I will ignore the aggressive tone and focus on their bidding pattern. They consistently demand 'valueA' for issueA and recently proposed 'valueD' for issueB, which works well for me. By conceding issueD from 'valueD' to 'valueC', I can signal significant flexibility while maintaining a strong overall utility around 0.73.

The opponent's messages consist of standard aggressive posturing, claiming my offers are "insulting" without pointing to specific constraints. However, their physical bids show a consistent desire for `issueA: valueA` and some flexibility on other issues, occasionally offering `issueB: valueC`, `issueC: valueC`, and `issueD: valueB`. I will continue conceding on my lowest-weight issue (`issueA`) to their preferred value, and propose a new compromise mixing `valueC` and `valueD` for to maintain my utility around 0.75 while exploring permutations they might accept.

The opponent's aggressive messages are treated as cheap talk, but their actual offers reveal they are willing to concede on issue A (valueC), issue B (valueD), and issue C (valueC) at various times. To find a mutually acceptable package now that time is advancing past the halfway mark (t=0.64), I will combine these previously offered values while dropping my ask on issue D slightly to valueC. This preserves a strong utility of approximately 0.78 for me while moving towards their demonstrated constraints.

The current time is 0.66, entering the later stages of negotiation. The opponent's messages are highly aggressive, but their actual offers show a willingness to concede on specific issues, such as proposing `valueD` for `issueB` which aligns perfectly with my preferences. Knowing this, I will formally concede `issueA` entirely to their favored `valueA` and maintain our mutually beneficial `valueD` on `issueB`, while demanding strong values on my heavily weighted issues (`issueC` and `issueD`) to maintain an overall utility above 0.80.

The opponent's abusive language and complaints about lowballing are clear tactical posturing. Looking strictly at their bid history, their latest offer (issueA: valueA, issueB: valueC, issueC: valueC, issueD: valueC) comes closer to my needs on issueC and issueD. Since they have recently shown willingness to offer 'valueD' on issueB (at t=0.60 and t=0.66) and just conceded on C and D, I will combine these into an offer that maximizes my utility for the remaining issues. I'll propose a split giving them 'valueA' on my least important issue (issueA) while seeking a combination of their best recently-offered terms for the others, targeting an overall utility ~0.73.

The opponent's messages are highly aggressive and manipulative, using fake anger as a negotiation tactic, but their offers show a genuine pattern of concessions. Specifically, their prior offer at t=0.68 incorporated 'valueC' across three issues. Since we are at t=0.7, I will make a concession by proposing a variation close to their recent bids, matching 'valueC' on issueB, issueC, and issueD while asking for 'valueB' on issueA to maintain a firm utility around 0.70.

The negotiation is entering the late stage (t=0.72), and the opponent hasn't significantly increased the utility of their offers, continuously proposing combinations that keep my overall utility lower than desired while spamming repetitive aggressive messages meant to simulate artificial pressure. I will focus on finding a concession that stays above my target utility (~0.70) while making room for the opponent on lower-weight issues like issueA and issueB. Offering a tradeoff with valueA on issueA, which the opponent frequently targets, and securing higher-value returns on my more vital issues C and D might finally push us toward agreement.
